title: podman generated coredump in a stress env when creating nested containers.
body:
### Issue Description

Using podman-5.8.0-2.el10 in a memory stress scenario resulted in a coredump.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Create a container
2. Ensure the machine is consuming ~90% of total memory
3. Create a nested container

To simplify this, we used [https://github.com/containers/engine-stressor](https://github.com/containers/engine-stressor), which is essentially a wrapper around `stress-ng`. The arguments used are below (note that `--vm-bytes` is set to twice the total system memory):

```
stress-ng \
  --vm 8 \
  --vm-bytes 28406M \
  --oom-avoid \
  --vm-method write64 \
  --timeout 900s
```


### Describe the results you received

comments.body:
> [github.com/modern-go/reflect2.(*safeType).AssignableTo(0x2003790c0]([http://github.com/modern-go/reflect2.(*safeType).AssignableTo(0x2003790c0)](https://github.com/modern-go/reflect2.(*safeType).AssignableTo(0x2003790c0))?, {0xaaaad8421f60?, 0x40004104e0?})

That is not in podman code so there isn't much we could do about it.

Seems like that code is called from json-iterator which is also unmaintained. We had some disscusion for a while to move away from json-iterator so that would solve it but so far we have been waiting on the new json/v2 std API as replacement which is still marked as experimental so not sure we like to adopt it yet until it is called stable.

I think it is a memory corruption problem that could happen almost anywhere in the code, caused by low free memory. Using a more memory-efficient JSON library would definitely help, but I have a feeling the problem could be somewhere else. Maybe using a different swap size or something similar would work, but it would require more experimentation.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
